metadata.summary: Identify stakeholders, map them on Power/Interest grid, assign engagement strategies per quadrant, execute cadence in version-controlled register for auditable history and evidence-backed attitudes.

content.title: Stakeholder Engagement
content.summary: A structured process to identify all parties affected by or affecting a project, map each on the Power/Interest grid, assign an engagement strategy per quadrant, and execute a communication cadence — stored as version-controlled YAML register so changes produce a diff history and evidence-backed attitudes. Engagement is continuous; register must refresh quarterly minimum.

why.p: Projects fail when key stakeholders are ignored or mismanaged. Missed requirements from silent stakeholders, resistance from influential parties, scope creep from unmanaged expectations, and approval bottlenecks all trace back to a broken or absent engagement plan. The Power/Interest grid is the minimum viable model; the Mitchell-Agle-Wood salience overlay (power × legitimacy × urgency) is required when political risk is significant.

list.item: Project kickoff for any cross-functional initiative with more than 5 named parties
Programs with significant political risk: M&A integration, reorgs, vendor consolidation, regulated rollouts
Multi-stakeholder transformation programs (cloud migration, ERP) with sponsors, champions, blockers, and external auditors
Public-facing programs (government, NGO, infrastructure) with citizen/community stakeholders
Pre-RFP or vendor-selection efforts where the buying committee has hidden influencers
Solo founders or very small teams (under 5 stakeholders) — direct conversation beats matrix overhead
One-off internal hotfixes or refactors with no business stakeholder change — RACI alone is enough
Anonymous open-source community projects — power/interest axes are meaningless without identity
Crisis or incident response — incident command structure replaces engagement plan during P0
When stakeholder identification is still unsolved — do BA stakeholder-analysis first to get a register

section.name: five-step-process
section.p: The engagement process follows five sequential steps from identification through monitoring and adaptation.

list.item: Step 1: Identify all stakeholders — sponsor, users, customers, team, regulators, suppliers, influencers. Seed from org chart, charter, RFP, and contract.
Step 2: Analyze Power/Interest — map each stakeholder on the 2x2 grid; apply Mitchell-Agle-Wood (power × legitimacy × urgency) for political risk.
Step 3: Plan engagement strategies — one strategy per quadrant; override only with explicit rationale.
Step 4: Execute communication plan — cadence, channel, owner, content per stakeholder.
Step 5: Monitor and adapt — refresh quarterly minimum; triangulate self-reported attitude with behavioural signals.

section.name: quadrant-strategies
section.p: The Power/Interest grid creates four quadrants, each requiring different engagement tactics.

rule.statement: Apply the default cadence by quadrant: Manage Closely = weekly 1:1, Keep Satisfied = biweekly summary, Keep Informed = monthly digest, Monitor = release notes only.
rule.rationale: Default cadence is empirically calibrated to stakeholder tolerance; deviating without rationale causes either fatigue (over-engagement) or surprises (under-engagement).

list.item: High Power + High Interest (Manage Closely): weekly meetings, direct updates, real-time dashboard access, early warning on delays.
High Power + Low Interest (Keep Satisfied): monthly briefings, escalation path, fast response to queries, no unnecessary meetings.
Low Power + High Interest (Keep Informed): newsletters, status reports, sprint demos where relevant.
Low Power + Low Interest (Monitor): general announcements, release notes.

section.name: attitude-model
section.p: Attitudes capture the stakeholder's current disposition toward the project or initiative. Evidence is mandatory.

rule.statement: Require an evidence field on every attitude assertion — no evidence means attitude = "unknown", not "supportive".
rule.rationale: LLMs over-classify everyone as "supportive" by default (politeness bias). Forcing evidence eliminates false positives that lead to missed resistors.

rule.statement: Triangulate self-reported attitude with at least one behavioural signal: meeting attendance, response time, escalations, or Slack sentiment.
rule.rationale: People say "supportive" in workshops and undermine in private; behavioural signals are less gameable than self-report.

antipattern.description: Treating all Manage Closely stakeholders with the same tone and cadence.
antipattern.reason: A CFO and a regulator demand opposite communication styles even though both fall in the same quadrant — context-specific strategy is required.

section.name: hidden-stakeholder-review
section.p: Hidden stakeholders are systematically omitted from initial org charts and are the top source of late-stage blockers.

rule.statement: Always include a hidden stakeholder pass: legal, infosec, procurement, works council/union, accessibility, downstream API consumers.
rule.rationale: Systematically missed stakeholders are the top source of late-stage blockers in enterprise programs; they never appear in org-chart exports.

section.name: register-as-code
section.p: Storing the stakeholder register in version-controlled YAML creates an auditable history and enforces governance.

rule.statement: Store the register in git (YAML), not a wiki — diffs become the engagement history; CODEOWNERS gate sponsor sign-off on attitude changes.
rule.rationale: Wiki pages have no diff history, no access control per field, and no CI integration. Git provides all three and makes the history auditable.

section.p: The register (stakeholders/register.yaml) feeds a generated engagement-plan.md. The plan never auto-sends to executives — it drafts; humans deliver.

section.name: recommended-subagents
section.p: An effective agentic workflow breaks engagement curation into specialised agents, each with clear scope and model selection.

list.item: register-curator-agent (sonnet): ingests org chart, kickoff transcripts, RFP; emits patch with new/changed entries; never overwrites existing entries silently.
attitude-triangulator-agent (sonnet): cross-references self-reported attitude against meeting attendance, response time, escalations; flags drift.
engagement-planner-agent (sonnet): converts register quadrants into Step-5 engagement table with cadence, channel, owner, content.
salience-scorer-agent (opus): applies Mitchell-Agle-Wood (power × legitimacy × urgency); identifies definitive stakeholders requiring daily attention.
engagement-sentiment-agent (sonnet): listens to feedback channels and updates attitude column with evidence trail.
password-scrubber-agent: runs over engagement docs before commit; the register frequently leaks salaries, customer names, candid commentary.

section.name: register-curator-prompt-pattern
section.p: The register curator uses a strict JSON patch format that prevents silent overwrites and enforces evidence requirements.

example.note: Strict JSON patch — prevents overwrite, enforces evidence requirement:

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: LLMs have systematic biases in stakeholder analysis. These gotchas represent real deployment failures.

antipattern.description: Agent invents stakeholder names not found in org-chart or charter inputs.
antipattern.reason: LLMs hallucinate plausible-sounding personas; always seed with real org-chart export and reject any name not in the source list unless flagged suggested_to_verify.

antipattern.description: Agent sends engagement communications directly to executives without human review.
antipattern.reason: Auto-send is forbidden to executives, regulators, customers, board. Agent drafts; sponsor or PM approves and sends.

antipattern.description: Bulk reclassification of stakeholder quadrants in a single agent run.
antipattern.reason: Cap agent edits to 5 stakeholders per run and require human review on the diff — bulk changes destroy the evidence trail and can reverse carefully built relationships.

list.item: Mandatory human checkpoints: adding a stakeholder, changing attitude, escalating to sponsor, dropping a stakeholder from active engagement.
Stakeholder data is regulated PII (GDPR Art. 9 if attitude implies political opinion). Never send the register to a third-party model without DPA.
Registers with more than 50 stakeholders blow context — page by category (Internal/External/Regulator) and operate on slices.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Command-line tools enable register automation and analysis in CI pipelines.

list.item: git + git log — native history of relationship changes; replaces hand-rolled engagement history
yq — read/patch register YAML in pipelines (apt install yq / brew install yq)
jq — aggregate JSON exports from CRM/HR systems
graphviz (dot) — render influence/impact graph and relationship network (apt install graphviz)
mermaid-cli (mmdc) — generate 2x2 quadrant chart from YAML as PNG/SVG (npm i -g @mermaid-js/mermaid-cli)
pandoc — convert register and engagement plan to PDF/DOCX for sponsor review (https://pandoc.org)
pre-commit — block commits to attitude/quadrant changes without evidence: field
gh / glab — mirror engagement actions as issues; CODEOWNERS gate sponsor sign-off
op (1Password CLI) — pull sensitive contact info without committing it to git

section.name: services-and-integrations
section.p: Enterprise systems and SaaS tools provide source data and distribution channels for engagement.

list.item: Microsoft Graph (Entra ID) — source of truth for internal org chart; required to seed register without inventing.
Workday HCM / BambooHR — authoritative for level, span of control, manager; used for influence inference.
Salesforce — authoritative for external (customer) stakeholders; REST + Bulk API.
HubSpot — lighter SMB CRM; webhooks drive attitude triggers.
Notion / Confluence — common register host; weak typing — schema-validate before commit.
Jira / Linear — Stakeholder = issue with custom fields (less ideal but common).
Smaply / UXPressia — Persona/journey tools; visual artifact, weak agent automation.
Mural / Miro / FigJam — workshop canvas for matrix exercises; agents can post sticky notes via API.
Slack / MS Teams — source for behavioural signal; use with explicit consent only.
SurveyMonkey / Typeform / Qualtrics — periodic stakeholder pulse surveys feeding attitude triangulation.
Calendly / SavvyCal — meeting load monitor for high-touch stakeholders (avoid burnout).

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Proven practices prevent common pitfalls in stakeholder engagement at scale.

rule.statement: One canonical register in git; if Workday or Salesforce holds source data, mirror nightly — do not maintain two truths.
rule.rationale: Two register sources diverge within weeks; the one not updated during a crisis is the one consulted, producing wrong engagement actions.

rule.statement: Treat the register as confidential — private repo, not a wiki page; run password-scrubber-agent before any commit.
rule.rationale: Stakeholder attitude data leaking to the wrong stakeholder terminates relationships; candid commentary is common in these files.

rule.statement: Calendar audits prevent burnout — if any stakeholder appears in more than 3 weekly meetings, consolidate.
rule.rationale: Well-meant high-touch engagement burns goodwill faster than under-engagement when it exceeds the recipient's tolerance.

list.item: Refresh quarterly minimum, monthly for volatile contexts (post-merger, leadership change, regulator activity).
Triangulate self-reported attitude with at least one behavioural signal (attendance, Slack sentiment, response time, escalations).
Apply the Mitchell-Agle-Wood salience overlay for political risk — flag "definitive" (power × legitimacy × urgency) stakeholders for daily attention.
Tie cadence to quadrant by default: Manage Closely = weekly, Keep Satisfied = biweekly, Keep Informed = monthly, Monitor = release-only. Override only with rationale.
Always include a "hidden stakeholders" review: legal, infosec, procurement, works council/union, accessibility, downstream API consumers.
Pair stakeholder weight with requirements-prioritization so high-influence support amplifies a requirement's score.
Engagement plan is a living document with explicit ownership per stakeholder; rotate owners only on transition.

section.name: pairing-with-related-methodologies
section.p: Stakeholder engagement is most effective when integrated with complementary practices in communications, change, and risk.

list.item: Pair with communications-management/ (executable form of the engagement plan), stakeholder-register/, stakeholder-engagement-advanced/, change-control/, and risk-register.
Pair with business-analyst stakeholder-analysis/ for the initial discovery phase if stakeholder identification is incomplete.
Tie cadence to quadrant in the communications plan so one source of truth drives all engagement actions.
Use the SDD-executor-agent to drive engagement as formal tasks: TASK_register_baseline, TASK_engagement_plan, TASK_cadence_setup, TASK_quarterly_refresh.

section.name: limitations
section.p: Be aware of the boundaries and failure modes inherent in stakeholder analysis methods.

list.item: Power/Interest 2x2 flattens multi-dimensional politics (urgency, legitimacy, salience). Mitchell-Agle-Wood overlay is needed for serious political risk.
Static analysis goes stale fast (2–6 weeks in dynamic orgs); a register that is not refreshed is wrong, not just old.
Self-reported attitude is unreliable — people say "supportive" in workshops and undermine in private. Triangulate against behaviour.
Hidden stakeholders (works councils, security, procurement, downstream consumers) are systematically missed unless seeded with org-chart context.
LLMs invent plausible-sounding personas that do not exist in the target organisation; outputs must validate against directory data.
Cross-cultural blindness: high-context cultures (JP, KR, parts of EU/MEA) route influence through indirect networks the matrix does not represent.
Engagement strategies that treat all "Manage Closely" stakeholders the same fail — a CFO and a regulator demand opposite tone, cadence, and content.
Stakeholder fatigue: well-meant high-touch engagement burns goodwill if asks exceed the recipient's tolerance.

template.purpose: Stakeholder register schema: id, power, interest, attitude, quadrant, cadence, last_engaged
Script: flag stakeholders past their engagement cadence deadline
